handedness

Author

Pete Nelson

introduction

Imagine that you’re in a room of 100 people, and you’re trying to pick out your fellow scientist in the crowd. You know that only 1% of the general population are scientists, so the odds of finding the scientist in this group by random selection is poor. You notice, however, the fellow signing his receipt at the bar is using his left hand, and you’ve heard that 20% of scientists are left-handed whereas only 10% of the greater population is left-handed. Gathering your courage, you tap the fellow on the shoulder saying, “Wonderful to find a fellow scientist here! Are you rooting for Pearson or Bayes?”

What are the odds that you’ve identified a kindred spirit?!

how to proceed

Without the information on handedness, your odds of having successfully identified the scientist are poor (maybe you went by the ratty Patagonia fleece), only 1%. Once you see evidence of handedness, however, Bayes’ Theorem allows you to “update” your confidence because a “lefty” is twice as likely to be a scientist than a non-scientist, so your probability of success greatly increased. But, you still have to be careful: the vast majority of lefties are still going to be non-scientists.

data

Let’s re-create your situation in the form of a dataset wherein (for illustration purposes) the single scientist is, indeed, a lefty.

library(tidyverse)
library(brms)

# create a small dataset reflecting our 'prior' and 'evidence'
# 100 people: 1 Scientist (Left-handed), 99 Non-Scientists (10 are Lefties)

data <-
  tibble(
    is_scientist = c(1, rep(0, 99)), # 1=scientist, 0=non-scientist
    is_lefty = c(1, rep(1, 10), rep(0, 89)) # 1=left, 0=non-lefty
  )

define the model

Now, we’ll define a model to calculate the odds that you’ve correctly identified the scientist, incorporating your knowledge of the scarcity of scientists and the frequency of left-handedness.

# define a prior for the intercept (the 'base rate' of being a scientist)
# a value of -4.6 on the logit scale is roughly 1%

my_prior <- set_prior("normal(-4.6, 0.5)", class = "Intercept")

# fit the model
fit <- brm(
  is_scientist ~ is_lefty, 
  data = data, 
  family = bernoulli(link = "logit"),
  prior = my_prior,
  chains = 4, iter = 2000, seed = 42,
  # keep Stan from chattering at you (sometimes a good thing)
  silent = 2
)

Note: The logit scale and the Bernoulli family are standard tools for modeling binary outcomes (heads vs tails, survived vs croaked, etc.). The regression-based model we’re using is happy to work with any number from negative infinity to positive infinity, but our data are either 0 or 1. And our probabilities can only be allowed to range from 0 to 1 (one of our President’s many misunderstandings). By telling brms to use the Bernoulli distribution, we’re telling our model to estimate the probability of “success” (i.e., finding the scientist) for each individual in the room.

analysis

Your model output includes a posterior probability distribution that can provide an estimate of the likelihood you’ve successfully identified your fellow scientist:

# predict the probability of being a scientist, given he's a lefty (is_lefty = 1)
new_data <- data.frame(is_lefty = 1)
posterior_probs <- posterior_epred(fit, newdata = new_data)

# show the average updated probability
mean(posterior_probs)
[1] 0.095943

So…our model has just calculated the odds that you’ve successfully identified the sole scientist in the crowd at approximately 10%. Maybe these still aren’t betting odds, but they’re still much better than what you started with.

more on the model output

print(fit)
 Family: bernoulli 
  Links: mu = logit 
Formula: is_scientist ~ is_lefty 
   Data: data (Number of observations: 100) 
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
          Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept    -5.04      0.52    -6.08    -4.04 1.00     1693     1747
is_lefty      2.30      1.49    -1.08     4.74 1.00     1779     1762

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).
plot(fit)

Here’s how to interpret this output:

metric value meaning
Estimate 2.37 the best guess for the increase in log-odds (logit scale)
Est.Error 1.46 standard deviation of your posterior distribution; how “spread out” your belief is
lower 95% CI -0.97 we aren’t 100% sure; there could be a slight negative effect
upper 95% CI 4.78 …or it could be a massive positive effect (>100 times more likely)
Exp(Estimate) 10.7x convert Estimate to a more intuitive metric by exponeniating your probability estimate: e2.37 = approximately 10.7

We may have hoped that the confidence intervals did not span 0, but our sample size, frankly, is pretty small, only 100 people, so this is really just an honest reflection of our uncertainty. The model has shifted its belief significantly toward “Scientist” based on that one lefty in your data, even though the starting assumption was that scientists are very rare.

team Pearson

What if you were strongly Team Pearson? Would your statistical accumen have helped you avoid embarrassment?

Here’s the frequentist approach to the problem. For this we use a type of GLM (generalized linear model) called a logistic regression. While brms used Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) to estimate distributions, a logistic regression will use maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) to find the single “best” set of parameters that make your observed data most likely.

# frequentist logistic regression
freq_fit <- glm(is_scientist ~ is_lefty, 
                data = data, 
                # Bernoulli, used in the Bayesian ver, is a special case of the Binomial
                # so this is approximately the same
                family = binomial(link = "logit"))

summary(freq_fit)

Call:
glm(formula = is_scientist ~ is_lefty, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
    data = data)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept)   -22.57    5108.78  -0.004    0.996
is_lefty       20.26    5108.78   0.004    0.997

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 11.200  on 99  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  6.702  on 98  degrees of freedom
AIC: 10.702

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 21

What?! How do you interpret this?

compare results

Let’s compare these results…

library(broom)
library(broom.mixed)

# tidy up the frequentist model
freq_results <- tidy(freq_fit, conf.int = TRUE) %>% 
  mutate(model = "Frequentist (GLM)")

# tidy up the Bayesian model
bayesian_results <- tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE) %>% 
  mutate(model = "Bayesian (brms)")

# bind them together
comparison <- bind_rows(freq_results, bayesian_results) %>%
  select(model, term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high)

print(comparison)
# A tibble: 4 × 5
  model             term        estimate conf.low conf.high
  <chr>             <chr>          <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 Frequentist (GLM) (Intercept)   -22.6  -1946.      152.  
2 Frequentist (GLM) is_lefty       20.3  -1050.       NA   
3 Bayesian (brms)   (Intercept)    -5.04    -6.08     -4.04
4 Bayesian (brms)   is_lefty        2.30    -1.08      4.74

Note the frequentist intercept estimate (-22.6) and extreme range of the confidence interval (-1946 to 152), and the NA at one extreme for the is_lefty term. What about the frequentist is_lefty term of 20.3?! This is absurd…the model is suggesting that a lefty is 650 million times more likely to be a scientist and a mathematical artifact of too little data:

e20.3 = (about) 650,000,000

The GLM simply can’t handle the rarity of the scientist and the model is unstable. The Bayesian model is able to provide a sane, usable interval and a realistic estimate. Team Pearson has it’s strengths, but in this case Team Bayes is the clear winner. To really hammer this home, let’s compare these results graphically.

library(modelr)
library(tidybayes)

# create a new person who is a lefty
new_person <- data.frame(is_lefty = 1)

# frequentist prediction (Single Point)
freq_pred <- predict(freq_fit, newdata = new_person, type = "response")

# Bayesian prediction (Distribution of possibilities)
bayesian_pred <- fit %>%
  add_epred_draws(newdata = new_person) %>%
  summarize(mean_prob = mean(.epred), 
            lower_ci = quantile(.epred, 0.025), 
            upper_ci = quantile(.epred, 0.975))
# compare them
print(
  tibble("Frequentist Prob" = round(freq_pred, 3),
         "Bayesian Prob" = round(bayesian_pred$mean_prob, 3))
)
# A tibble: 1 × 2
  `Frequentist Prob` `Bayesian Prob`
               <dbl>           <dbl>
1              0.091           0.096

Looking at the estimates from both models, there doesn’t appear to be much difference, but let’s plot the two distributions:

# 1. extract Bayesian draws for the 'is_lefty' coefficient
bayesian_draws <- fit %>%
  gather_draws(b_is_lefty) %>%
  mutate(model = "Bayesian (brms)")

# 2. create a normal distribution for the Frequentist model
# centered at 2.37 with SD of 1.46 (using your Bayesian results)
freq_x <- seq(-2, 7, length.out = 100)
freq_dist <- data.frame(
  .value = freq_x,
  density = dnorm(freq_x, mean = 2.37, sd = 1.46),
  model = "Frequentist (GLM)"
)

# 3. plot both
ggplot() +
  # Bayesian Density (The mountain)
  stat_density(data = bayesian_draws, aes(x = .value, fill = model), 
               alpha = 0.5) +
  # Frequentist Normal Curve
  geom_line(data = freq_dist, aes(x = .value, y = density, color = model), 
            linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  # add a line at 0 to show the "significance" threshold
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Bayesian Posterior vs. Frequentist Distribution",
       x = "Effect Size (Log-Odds of being a Scientist)",
       y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

but wait!

It still doesn’t look like there’s much difference. If the frequentist results were so crazy, why do these curves look so similar? The reason is that we plotted the Bayesian model parameters using a normal (Gaussian) distribution (look at line 11 in the code chunk above). In other words, we compared the shapes of these alternatives. Here’s what it looks like if you plot the actual outputs of each model:

# 1. extract the actual Bayesian draws
bayesian_draws <- fit %>%
  gather_draws(b_is_lefty) %>%
  mutate(model = "Bayesian (brms)")

# 2. use the your ACTUAL GLM results (Estimate: 20.3, Error: ~1050)
# Note: since our output had NA for some errors, we'll use 1050 as a conservative 'minimum' disaster
freq_x <- seq(-100, 100, length.out = 1000) 
freq_dist <- data.frame(
  .value = freq_x,
  density = dnorm(freq_x, mean = 20.3, sd = 1050), 
  model = "Frequentist (GLM) - Exploded"
)

# 3. plot both outputs
ggplot() +
  # the Bayesian "Mountain" now looks like a needle because we've zoomed out so far
  stat_density(data = bayesian_draws, aes(x = .value, fill = model), 
               alpha = 0.6) +
  # the frequentist "Pancake"
  geom_line(data = freq_dist, aes(x = .value, y = density, color = model), 
            linewidth = 1) +
  # Zoom out to see the scale of the failure
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-50, 50)) + 
  labs(title = "The Bayesian 'Needle' vs. The Frequentist 'Pancake'",
       subtitle = "why priors matter for small samples",
       x = "Effect Size (Log-Odds)",
       y = "Density") +
  theme_bw()

In the Bayesian model, we used an informative prior to say that, yes, my left-handed friend is indeed a scientist, but I’m not going to go overboard and I’ll keep things reasonable. The frequentist model said, in effect, “OMG! I have seen exactly one scientist. That scientist is left-handed. I have seen zero scientists who are right-handed. Therefore, the ‘most likely’ state of the universe is that all scientists are lefties and all lefties are scientists.”

In statistics, this is called “The Problem of the Zero Cell.” Because there are zero scientists who are right-handed in your experience (data), the frequentist math (which involves dividing by counts) effectively tries to divide by zero to find the ratio.

The Bayesian approach avoids this “explosion” because the prior acts like “pseudo-data”—it’s as if the model has already seen thousands of right-handed non-scientists before it even looked at your room of 100 people.

challenge

Try adding 10 more right-handed non-scientists to the crowd. How does that affect the outcome? What happens if you add just one right-handed scientist to the crowd?!
